title: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
author: Rachel Burkey
orientation: Rachel Burkey recounts witnessing two abolitionists at the Missouri Baptist convention vote in the minority against a pro-life resolution that praised compromise with abortion regulation, framing their refusal to spin defeat as victory as a model of 'righteous intransigence' that fears God above man.
audiences: Pro-life Christian considering whether 'incremental wins' are worth celebrating
Abolitionist activist coming off a public defeat at a denominational meeting
Pastor or church leader trying to influence convention resolutions
Skeptic wondering whether refusing to compromise is just stubbornness
Young Christian deciding which leaders to look up to and emulate

questions.question: When is intransigence a virtue rather than a vice, according to this article?
questions.audience: Skeptic wondering whether refusing to compromise is just stubbornness
questions.answer: Burkey argues that uncompromising stubbornness becomes a duty when it concerns holding to scripture as the sole rule of faith and refusing to compromise with what she calls modern-day child sacrifice. In those domains compromise is not merely strategically foolish but morally offensive to God.
questions.quote: In certain regards, however, this stubborn, uncompromising attitude is not only good, but it is our duty. For example, we must be intransigent in holding to the scriptures as our sole infallible rule of faith. When it comes to the fight against modern-day child sacrifice, compromising with our culture of death is not merely a foolish move strategically, but it is a stench in God’s nostrils.

questions.question: What was the abolitionist resolution at the Missouri Baptist convention actually asking the legislature to do?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader trying to influence convention resolutions
questions.answer: The Missouri Baptists for the Abolition of Abortion Resolution called the state legislature to enact only just, righteous, and God-fearing legislation on abortion and to repent of its previous pro-life compromise that approved legalized, regulated abortion.
questions.quote: The goal was to pass an abolitionist resolution on abortion that was consistent with God’s Word, calling the state legislature to only enact just, righteous, and God-fearing legislation on abortion, and to repent of their previous pro-life compromise and approval of legalized, regulated child sacrifice.

questions.question: Why does Burkey refuse to count a 'strongest pro-life resolution ever' as a real win?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering whether 'incremental wins' are worth celebrating
questions.answer: Even though the abolitionist pressure produced the strongest pro-life language Missouri Baptists had ever adopted, the final resolution still praised and celebrated compromise with abortion regulation. She compares treating that as victory to perfuming a manure pile, because dressing up compromise with iniquity does not stop it from stinking before God.
questions.quote: But these men were not interested in decorating the manure pile of compromise with iniquity, the regulation of abortion. They were not attracted by throwing perfume bombs on the stench of child sacrifice that rises to God’s nostrils. Instead, they were calling the convention to repentance.

questions.question: How should an abolitionist behave after investing months in an effort that ends in public defeat?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist coming off a public defeat at a denominational meeting
questions.answer: Rather than spinning the loss into a personal win or taking credit for moving the needle, the two men Burkey watched cast minority votes against the compromised resolution and continued to call the convention to repentance. They cared more about God’s assessment than their reputation among brothers and friends who took their stance as an insult.
questions.quote: But these men cared more about what God thought of them than their own reputations among men. They concerned themselves first and foremost with standing on the Word of God, even when their brothers, sisters, and friends took their position as an insult, and forced them to stand alone in a room full of professing Christians.

questions.question: Whom does Burkey say Christians should choose as role models, and on what basis?
questions.audience: Young Christian deciding which leaders to look up to and emulate
questions.answer: She warns against worldly criteria like accolades, fame, accomplishments, age, or perceived success and instead urges Christians to honor those who refuse to abandon God’s Word and walk with the multitude. The defining mark of a Christian life worth imitating is righteous intransigence regardless of the personal cost.
questions.quote: We should point each other to examples like these, and look up to those who refuse to compromise with iniquity, regardless of the results. We likewise must hold fast to a biblical position even when it means standing against the hundreds of professing Christians around us. Our lives as children of God must be marked with righteous intransigence, whatever sacrifices we may face as a result.

questions.question: What does Burkey think the convention’s rejection of the abolitionist amendment revealed about the body?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader trying to influence convention resolutions
questions.answer: She reads the vote as an expression of pride: the messengers were repulsed by the suggestion that they themselves had something to repent of for celebrating pro-life legislation that compromises with the culture of death, so they killed the amendment.
questions.quote: Long story short: the convention reacted in pride, repulsed at the thought that they might have something to repent of, and voted down the abolitionist amendment to their resolution.

related.title: Point of Order! How Missouri Baptists Killed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.
related.reason: Direct companion piece on the same Missouri Baptist convention fight over the abolitionist resolution Burkey describes here.

related.title: Moving Beyond Regulationism: A Response to David Norman
related.reason: Burkey’s longer theological case against compromising with the regulation of abortion, which is the very compromise she calls a 'manure pile' in this article.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the principled abolitionist objection to celebrating incremental pro-life wins that this article models in microcosm at a Baptist convention.

related.title: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
related.reason: Offers Q-and-A style rebuttals to the pro-life arguments that pressured the convention to praise rather than repent.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: A counter-example showing what an uncompromised, non-incremental Baptist convention resolution looks like when intransigence carries the day.

related.title: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
related.reason: Burkey’s later piece on Southern Baptist leadership attacking abolitionists, illustrating the 'slander and misrepresentations' she warns this article’s readers to expect.

related.title: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
related.reason: Helps the reader place the convention messengers, the committee, and the two intransigent abolitionists into a useful taxonomy.
